---
title: "The Evertum Hypothesis"
subtitle: "A Worked Example of the Quarto + LaTeX Authoring Format"
author: "Keldon"
date: 2026-06-01
# In the real book these settings live in shared _quarto.yml / metadata.yml.
# Quarto's crossref defaults are sensible; shown here only to illustrate
# how you would customize them. (Chapter-based numbering like "Equation 3.2"
# is enabled at the book level via `crossref: chapters: true`.)
crossref:
  sec-prefix: "§"        # default would be "Section"
link-citations: true
bibliography: references.bib
---

# Foundations {#sec-foundations}

This file is a *demonstration* of the agreed source format: plain Quarto
(Pandoc-flavored) Markdown for prose, LaTeX for the mathematics, stable explicit
IDs for cross-references, native theorem environments, and a raw-LaTeX "escape
hatch" for the few things Markdown cannot express. Everything below compiles to
HTML, PDF (via LaTeX), and EPUB from the *same* source.

Notice how readable the raw text stays — that is what keeps iteration fast,
diffs clean, and token usage low.

## Notation {#sec-notation}

We write the spacetime metric as $g_{\mu\nu}$, the scale factor as $a(t)$, and
use natural units where $c = \hbar = 1$ unless stated otherwise. Inline math is
just `$...$`: the Hubble parameter is $H \equiv \dot{a}/a$.

## The Governing Equation {#sec-governing}

The central relation of the model is the modified Friedmann equation:

$$
H^2 = \frac{8\pi G}{3}\,\rho \;+\; \frac{\Lambda_{\text{ev}}}{3}
       \;-\; \frac{k}{a^2}
$$ {#eq-friedmann}

Here $\rho$ is the energy density and $\Lambda_{\text{ev}}$ is the *evertum
term* introduced in @sec-foundations. Multi-line, aligned derivations use a
LaTeX `aligned` block, which Markdown alone cannot do cleanly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{\rho} + 3H(\rho + p) &= -\dot{\Lambda}_{\text{ev}} \\
                          &= -\,\gamma\, H\, \Lambda_{\text{ev}}
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-continuity}

Combining @eq-friedmann and @eq-continuity gives the result we rely on
throughout the rest of the book. Note that the reference numbers above update
*automatically* if you insert another equation earlier — you never renumber by
hand.

# Theorems and the LaTeX Escape Hatch {#sec-theorems}

Quarto has **native** theorem environments: a fenced div whose ID starts with
`thm-`, with the theorem's name given by a heading inside it. These auto-number
and render correctly in *both* HTML and PDF, so they are preferred over raw
LaTeX:

::: {#thm-conservation}
## Evertum Conservation

For any closed region $\Omega$ with boundary $\partial\Omega$, the evertum flux
satisfies

$$
\oint_{\partial\Omega} J^\mu \, dS_\mu = 0 .
$$
:::

You then reference it in prose as @thm-conservation, which renders as
"Theorem 1 (Evertum Conservation)" — portable across formats, no raw LaTeX
required.

For the genuinely LaTeX-only cases Quarto has no syntax for (custom TikZ
diagrams, bespoke environments), drop into a raw `` ```{=latex} `` block. It is
passed straight through to the PDF and ignored by non-LaTeX outputs, so pair it
with an HTML fallback when a web reader also needs to see something:

```{=latex}
% PDF-only: reaches LaTeX untouched, bypassing Markdown entirely.
\begin{quote}\itshape
  Raw LaTeX here is emitted verbatim into the PDF.
\end{quote}
```

# Figures, Tables, and Citations {#sec-assets}

Figures get a caption and a stable ID, so a reference like @fig-flux stays
correct forever:

![Schematic of evertum flux through a closed boundary.](figures/flux-diagram.png){#fig-flux width=70%}

As illustrated in @fig-flux, the net flux vanishes. A table works the same way:

| Symbol                | Meaning           | Units                  |
|-----------------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| $\Lambda_{\text{ev}}$ | Evertum term      | $[\text{length}]^{-2}$ |
| $\gamma$              | Coupling constant | dimensionless          |

: Key symbols used in this chapter. {#tbl-symbols}

See @tbl-symbols for definitions. Citations use bracketed `@` keys and pull from
a shared `.bib` file: the cosmological constant problem was framed by Weinberg
[@weinberg1989].[^note]

[^note]: Footnotes are first-class in Quarto Markdown — useful for asides
without breaking the flow of the argument.

# Cross-Chapter References {#sec-crossfile}

In a Quarto **book**, cross-references resolve *globally across files* at build
time. So even though (say) the entropy-bound discussion lives in a different
chapter file, you reference its label directly in prose — e.g. `@sec-entropy`
renders as a numbered, hyperlinked "§4.1" pointing at wherever that label is
defined, with no manual file paths and no fragile heading-text anchors.

(A plain Markdown link such as `[the appendix](appendix.qmd)` still works for
ordinary, non-cross-reference links.)
